creator: alfred@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to Andriy Gapon from comment #10)
> Another question that I'd like to ask is does any popular OS / OS
> distribution out there use the sample vimrc as the default vimrc?
> My impression from using several mainstream Linux distributions is that they
> use their own custom default vimrc files (installed under /tmp/vimrc,
> typically).
> And I have never encountered setting like those in the sample vimrc, esp.
> regarding the mouse integration.

I'm not sure what to say on this. The proposed fix is to ignore system vimrc/gvimrc if there is a ~/.g?vimrc that should suffice unless someone comes up with something else.

As far as "sensible defaults" I don't know, that could be a bikeshed that goes on for years.  The file is installed as .sample so you have options for overriding it.

> 
> Finally, regarding the message that accompanies the commit that introduced
> the POLA violation.  I do not think that vim actually operates in strict vi
> compatibility mode without any vimrc.  Also, arrow keys work as expected for
> me in vim without any vimrc.  Maybe terminal properties play a greater role
> with these.  However, it's true that syntax highlighting ("color mode") has
> to be explicitly enabled.

This is incorrect, you please must read the fine manual page.  This is exactly the case.  Without a .vimrc AND without a /usr/local/etc/vimrc then vim goes into compat mode which breaks arrow keys on many OSes, including OS-X ssh and using virtualbox.  I have not tried vmware, but those are broken as well.

I may not have explained this, but I was trying to get FreeBSD more use as desktop here at the office (even in a virtual machine) and with vim broken by default it was a complete turn-off.  

Srrow keys and backspace should work inside of an editor by default because that is all they know.

Please retry your experiments with no vimrc and you will see the problem. 

I will check in periodically on this, I'm assuming that under 1 week will be sufficient to get votes in on the matter so a decision can be made.
